course_title: Film and Television History: From the Cinematograph to CGI and Netflix

learning_outcomes.description: Grasp theoretical approaches and scholarly debates used to identify, explain, and analyse changes and continuities in film and TV form, style, and viewer experience over time; and apply and test these explanations against new examples.

Understand how film and TV industries, creators, and audiences have driven, rejected, negotiated, or responded to technological changes across different historical periods and contexts, and be able to identify and explain recurring patterns and anomalies in these responses.
Design and develop an independent film and TV history research project, and navigate digital databases and library search systems.
Apply skills in locating, contextualising, analysing, and evaluating primary sources; and synthesise this analysis into clear, evidence-based written arguments.

Apply skills in close viewing and listening to identify significant formal and stylistic elements in screen works; and clearly describe these elements to support arguments in written or video assignments.
Have experience participating in weekly group discussions, and engaging productively with the perspectives, observations, and ideas of others.

assessments.task_description:
One mark is available each week for seminar participation/engagement, for a total of ten marks over ten weeks. To receive the mark each week: write your name on the class sign-in sheet in the seminar room AND EITHER speak into the microphone (raise your hand, and I will bring it to you), OR write a response to a prompt or question on the seminar slides (or ask questions about the seminar topic) in the discussion space on blackboard. Discussion posts must engage with specific points from the seminar. Broad statements about a topic that do not refer to something specific from the seminar will receive a ‘0’.

If you are unable to attend the seminar due to a timetable clash, illness, or other good reason, email the course co-ordinator, Lisa, and she will arrange an alternative engagement activity so you can still earn the mark.

It is your responsibility to ensure your name is recorded on the sign-in sheet in class so that your participation can be marked, or email Lisa l.bode@uq.edu.au if you cannot make it to class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In-class: sign the attendance sheet in the seminar room, and either speak into the microphone during the seminar or post in the discussion space on Blackboard. The Blackboard discussion for the week closes 10 minutes after the conclusion of the seminar.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.weighting: <dd>40% 5 marks x 10 weeks. Best 8 for final mark out of 40 </dd>
assessments.task_description:
Each week in tutorials (starting week 2) there will be a timed writing exercise that must be completed on the provided worksheet (you are responsible for bringing your own pen or pencil). Students are not permitted to access devices of any kind during the timed task, but may consult printed or handwritten notes. You will be given 15 minutes preparation time, and 15 minutes writing time.

The exercises will be different each week. Earlier ones will be concerned with applying that week’s concept to a screen case study. The later ones will be focused on generating ideas and drafting parts of the final assignment (the screening program or video essay with written introduction). This gives you the option to complete part of the work for the final assignment in workshops, receive marks for that draft work, as well as receive feedback as you progress.

Feedback will be given informally. Your tutor will collect the worksheets afterwards to record completion of the assessment task. Each writing task will be marked out of 5. Your best 8 workshop exercises will count towards your final mark out of 40. To check this grade during the semester, please ask your tutor.

Students who miss a number of classes due to illness or other factors beyond their control will be given a chance to do a makeup session in week 13.
assessments.submission_guidelines: in-class writing tasks are to be completed in person each week in class, using paper and pen, and submitted to the tutor at the end of the class.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Cultural Reception and Screen Analysis project
assessments.task_description: This final assessment product allows you to demonstrate, think through, and apply what you have learned about the ways audiences, critics, and film and TV creatives have engaged with new production and/or distribution technologies in different historical moments. We are interested in how new technologies, ideas about the identity of the medium, aesthetic norms and creative practices interact and change over time. This assignment requires you to combine analysis of both screen examples and reception / promotion / "making-of" production materials. More information will be provided in class and on blackboard. The last few in-class assessments will help you get started and serve to scaffold this more complex assignment. You will be able to present your work in written form with images OR as a video essay with a written introduction.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1: Introductory Seminar

Thinking about Film and TV History.

*It is vital that you attend this first seminar, otherwise you will find it very difficult to catch up*

In preparation for this first class, please watch Singin' in The Rain (Donen/Kelly, 1952) and do the reading for Week 1. Both of these will be in the Week 1 subfolder of the "Course Content" folder on Blackboard Ultra.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05, L06

Both of these will be in the Week 1 subfolder of the "Course Content" folder on Blackboard Ultra.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 1 Workshop:

Please ensure you make this first week as it is crucial for learning particular research skills that you will need to find historical reception materials for the major assignment. Learn how to become a database whisperer and unlock buried film/TV history treasures that will help you complete the assignment successfully.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 2 Seminar: Early Cinema, early possibilities

This week we look at early cinema forms (1894-1910s), their expressive possibilities, some of the early writing on cinema, and how the shift from short 'attractions' films to narrative cinema has been explained by historians.

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 4 Seminar: The Emergence of Film Studios

This week we examine the rise to dominance of the Hollywood studio system in the USA in the wake of WWI, and studios elsewhere. We look at studios as both infrastructure (building complexes that allow for the control of filming environments and the centralisation of labour) and as production companies. Our big questions are: what impact did the emergence of the Hollywood studios have on the way films looked? To what extent have the studios driven technological transformation in filmmaking? Can what are essentially filmmaking factories produce art?

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 5 Seminar: The Transition to Sound

This week we examine the late 1920s/early 1930s when the Hollywood studios embraced sound cinema. What kinds of filmmaking expression were lost? What were the expressive possibilities and problems of sound film making? How were the first sound films received?

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6 Seminar: The Coming of Television

This week we look at how television was introduced to the home, and how different countries grappled with the problems and opportunities of the new medium.

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 7 Seminar: TV's 'First Golden Age' in the 1950s

This week looks at the aesthetics of early TV, its focus on liveness and the expressive possibilities of liveness, its move to pre-taped content, early discussions of what kind of artform TV might be, and its first "golden age".

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 8: Film fights back - colour, widescreen, 3-D

In this seminar we examine how and why in the 1950s cinema embraced a range of technologies to enhance its capacity for spectacle and viewer immersion. Which of these were temporary, and which became a permanent part of film-making, and why?

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 9: 1970s innovations in sound design, VFX, and cinematography

The 1970s are significant for giving us Dolby sound, steadicam, and motion-controlled camera. How were these technologies used expressively and to serve new styles of realism? What difference did they make to the way movies looked and sounded?

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

This week we look in some detail at how 1970s innovations in cameras, film speed, and sound recording and mixing had an impact on how films looked and sounded.

This week as well as discussing the week's topic in more depth, we will do the in-class writing assessment. Each one is written in pen on paper, and marked out of 5. Your best 8 tasks over the semester count towards a final mark out of 40.

learning_activities.topic:
Week 10 Seminar Before Netflix: VCR/DVD and the disruption of home viewing

This week we examine a key precursor to online streaming and Netflix. In the 1980s-early 1990s, long before online streaming services, the video cassette recorder disrupted the film and TV industries, transformed the relationship between viewers and content, and in turn shaped new kinds of TV programming and film-making.

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Seminar: The digital revolution - Part 1

As with the coming of sound, the rise of digital effects, computer 3D animation, and computer generated imagery (CGI) in film-making and television has been the subject of anxiety and excitement. How has it been written about? What do we lose, and what do we gain? What are the expressive possibilities of CGI?

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 11 Workshop:

This week we consider CGI remakes in comparison to their originals (made with pre-digital effects techniques) in order to analyse the difference (if any) that CGI makes to our impression of screen bodies, spaces, and actions.

This week as well as discussing the week's topic in more depth, we will do the in-class writing assessment. Each one is written in pen on paper, and marked out of 5. Your best 8 tasks over the semester count towards a final mark out of 40.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Seminar: The digital revolution Part 2 machine learning and Generative AI

This final week we look first at the wholesale digitisation of film and TV production in the 2000s, from being able to shoot a movie on your phone, to digital editing, the digital intermediate, and digital exhibition before turning to the rise of machine learning and image-generation technologies post-2017. How is AI being used in the industry? What kinds of debates and divisions are emerging in the industry? We have seen film change a great deal over the past 120 years. Is genAI just another technological evolution, or is it something fundamentally new?

In preparation for this week's class, please watch the screening and do the readings on Blackboard in the learning materials folder for this week. Participation and engagement is marked this week, so make sure you sign the sheet in class. If you cannot attend but still want to earn an engagement mark, please email Lisa for an alternative activity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06

How is AI being used in the industry? What kinds of debates and divisions are emerging in the industry? We have seen film change a great deal over the past 120 years. learning_activities.topic:
Week 12 Workshop

In this final workshop we look in more detail at the digitisation of production, the integration of machine learning and generative AI into filmmaking processes, and the rising schism in the film industry and screen culture.

This week as well as discussing the week's topic in more depth, we will do the in-class writing assessment. Each one is written in pen on paper, and marked out of 5. Your best 8 tasks over the semester count towards a final mark out of 40.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06
